palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is multi-creator past this stage, but the 'systemize before you scale' discipline maps onto Palm's THIN/GAP areas (acquisition §6, pricing §7, retention §9) that aren't yet documented.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Apply 'document and systemize before delegating/scaling' to close Palm's THIN areas before further growth.

new_findings.title: Use an Intrigue-Curiosity-CTA opener and book the call fast

variants.claim: Structure the first cold DM to intrigue, spark curiosity, and end with a clear call-to-action, then move to a call instead of selling in DMs.
variants.what_they_do: ICC framework: first 4-5 visible words must intrigue (no 'hey hope you're having a great day'), then say something that piques curiosity, then a CTA (reply / send number / book a call). Don't treat DMs as a Q&A; get to the point and get them on a call. Type every message manually, personalize lightly, space sends (~20-30/hr, ~400-500/day across many accounts), and follow up daily for up to 2 weeks ('the money is in the follow-up').

sources.quote: I stands for intrigue. C... stands for curiosity and the next C stands for call to action.
what you're trying to do is get their phone number and call them as soon as possible.

palm_comparison.vs_us: No documented DM script, send-rate discipline, or follow-up cadence for acquisition (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt an ICC opener template, a manual-typed/personalized/spaced sending SOP to avoid bans, and a 2-week daily follow-up rule; track per-step conversion.

new_findings.title: Qualify creators against a checklist and disqualify on red flags

variants.claim: Sign only creators who fit a defined ideal-client profile and screen out predictable red-flag situations rather than chasing anyone with a link in bio.
variants.what_they_do: Ideal client: already on OnlyFans, has/open to a niche, posts consistently, communicates, pays on time, has some IG/Twitter/TikTok/Reddit presence, and is coachable. Qualify briefly in DMs then on a call. Red flags that get declined or probed: under 3-4 months on OF, about to travel/move/have a baby, just had a breakup, churned through 5+ agencies recently, know-it-all who rejects all advice, asks zero questions, takes weeks to sign.

sources.quote: Here's my ideal client checklist. Number one, already on Only Fans... willingness to actually create content consistently... communication... payments... a bit coachable.
if they've gone through tons of agencies, especially in the last like 3 to 6 months... probably the common denominator is the creator.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding captures admin fields but has no documented fit scorecard or red-flag screen (§6 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add an ideal-client checklist plus a red-flag screen to the onboarding/intake step so Palm signs for fit and retention, not just availability.

new_findings.title: Anchor on a 50% full-service split, don't undersell at 20-30%

variants.claim: Charge around 50% for full-service management rather than discounting to a low percentage to win clients.
variants.what_they_do: Treats 50/50 as industry standard for doing everything (marketing + chatting); explicitly warns beginners against charging 20-30% out of desperation. Nuance: when starting with sub-$1k creators, stay around 40-60% (default 50%); higher 30-40% can fit only specific larger cases. Logic: getting a creator 1k->10k is harder than 37k->120k, so don't give away margin on the hard early work.

sources.quote: charging like 20, 30, 35% when you're just starting out... the industry standard, go with 50% and you can't go wrong with that.
a 50/50 deal is quite common in the only fans management industry.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding but has no documented split rationale/tiers (§7 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Write Palm's commission rationale with a 50% default anchor and explicit guidance on when to deviate, so splits aren't negotiated ad hoc.

new_findings.title: Build content around the creator's niche and her own strengths

variants.claim: Choose content by giving each creator a defined niche and building around her individual strengths, not by copying others' viral clips bar-for-bar.
variants.what_they_do: Calls 'no niche' the #1 agency mistake; with 4M+ creators you must stand out. For each creator, identify strengths/weaknesses (some are confident on camera, some shy-but-flexible) and create content that is easy for her to replicate and doesn't feel like work, rather than blindly recreating another creator's viral reel.

sources.quote: within literally one or two seconds I can see exactly what's wrong, which is there is no niche.
you must understand what are their strengths and weaknesses and play into their strengths. create content that is simple for them to do.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline is data-driven and mature for ideation (§1), but niche/positioning and creator-strength fit are not explicit fields in the flow.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a niche + creator-strengths step feeding the inspo pipeline so promoted ideas are filtered for fit, not just virality.

new_findings.title: Spin up backup/secondary accounts and post across many platforms

variants.claim: Multiply reach by creating multiple secondary social accounts and posting across many platforms, then funnel all of it to OnlyFans.
variants.what_they_do: Beyond the creator's main IG, create 2nd/3rd/4th IG accounts plus Reddit, Snapchat, threads, Facebook; even if a backup only adds 15k-100k views/mo it yields net-new paid subs you can attribute with a tracking link — distinct from the creator's existing audience. Post daily/multiple times, organize content a week in advance.

sources.quote: are you actually creating new accounts? Like... a second, third, fourth, fifth Instagram account... an extra 15, 20, 30, 100,000 views per month, it's better than nothing.
you can have a tracking link and all of a sudden you can show the client... extra 20, 50, 100 paid subscribers just from these backup accounts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's distribution strategy (Reddit, multi-account, S4S) is an explicit GAP (§5); cadence is partially uncodified (§1). Note: this overlaps Palm's AI warmup multi-account capability (§3/§4).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a multi-account/multi-platform traffic strategy with tracking links for attribution and a week-ahead scheduling cadence; reuse AI-warmup account infrastructure.

new_findings.title: Onboard with a documented same-day process and a welcome video

variants.claim: Convert a signed creator into active service the same day via a documented onboarding process that also manages expectations.
variants.what_they_do: No-onboarding-process is called the #2 fatal mistake. Use a pre-recorded welcome video (so you don't repeat yourself) that sets expectations: when/where to message, how to pay invoices, what content is required; then connect OF to Infloww and run an audit. Act with Amazon-like speed — even a 1-2 day delay risks the creator leaving in the first days.

sources.quote: you must act with speed... you must have an entire sequence... on what happens as soon as the contract is signed.
we have a pre-recorded video... which explains everything... how to pay the invoices, what content do we require from them.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm onboarding is a form wizard → Creators table (§6); strong on contract automation but no documented expectation-setting welcome video or same-day activation SOP.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a standardized welcome video + same-day activation checklist to onboarding to set expectations and cut early churn.

new_findings.title: Run three departments (acquisition, marketing, chatting) each under a manager

variants.claim: Free the owner by splitting the agency into core departments, each run by a manager the owner reports through, instead of doing everything personally.
variants.what_they_do: Typically three departments — client acquisition, marketing, chatting — each with a manager (e.g. chatter manager, marketing director, acquisition manager). The owner checks in with managers (not every IC) once each morning; managers run the ICs (outreach VAs, editors, chatters). Beginners can start with 3-6 chatters + one chatter manager. Trying to do it all solo makes scaling to $1M/mo 'physically impossible.'

sources.quote: you want to have three four main departments... Client acquisition, marketing, and chatting... have a manager in client acquisition, a manager in marketing, and a manager in chatting.
once in the morning, you check in with all of the managers.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm org is THIN (§10): no management layer between founders and ICs, no org chart.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a department-and-manager org (acquisition / marketing / chatting) with the founder managing through managers as Palm scales past the current flat structure.

new_findings.title: Weigh AI's zero-follower cold start and believability problem before betting on it

variants.claim: Treat an AI creator's from-scratch follower build and 'too-perfect to be real' suspicion as core risks, and prefer scaling a real creator with existing momentum.
variants.what_they_do: Argues AI OFM is overhyped: content is limited (close-to-camera, props/Ferrari emblems look wrong, constant proof-checking, costs per generation), no real customs, no collabs/podcasts/in-person viral content, and you start from zero followers where a '10/10' new account looks suspicious. His own AI creator scaled to only ~$25k with huge effort; he prefers taking 50% of a real creator's fast growth over 100% of little.

sources.quote: you're going to be starting with an AI creator from scratch. like zero followers... how suspect it looks if your AI creator is like a 10 out of 10 chick.
we scaled a creator to $25,000. But the amount of time, energy, and effort that took. It is just not worth it compared to just working with real life creators.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm runs hardware-isolated AI warmup + EU AI Act labeling (§3/§4), which directly mitigates the cold-start/believability risk this raises; useful as a counter-view weighting Palm's real-first core (§0).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Keep real creators as the core and treat AI as a hedge; lean on Palm's warmup/labeling stack to de-risk the cold-start and believability problems this guru flags before scaling an AI line.

new_findings.title: Use the fan's name, kill mass-messages

variants.claim: Personalizing chats with the fan's name and avoiding generic mass-message bundle blasts is a high-leverage, underused driver of spend and LTV.
variants.what_they_do: Habibi stresses using the fan's actual name instead of 'babe' (people respond to their own name) and refuses mass-message bundle blasts and odd-number 'flea market' pricing, taking a new account from $33 to $500+ days with one-to-one personalized chatting.

sources.quote: people get aroused by their name... it's very underutilized even by chatters
we were sending no stupid... bundle deals... I don't advise mass messages at all

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM (§9 PARTIAL) but personalization data and tone live with outsourced chatters (§8). Build-toward plus owning the data.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Ensure Palm's own per-fan CRM captures the name and interests, and bar generic mass-message bundle blasts, so personalization survives any chatting-vendor change and feeds future AI chat.

new_findings.title: Price dynamically off purchase history

variants.claim: Setting price per fan from their purchase history, backing off after a no and pushing higher for proven whales, beats one fixed price for all.
variants.what_they_do: Luca Pritchard uses dynamic pricing read from each subscriber's unlock history via tools like Infloww: if a fan stalled at $65 he offers 17/27/47/59 next time, but if a fan will pay $150-$200 for what others pay $20 for he sells at that willingness, and he keeps explicit content premium-priced so perceived value stays high.

sources.quote: you pick a different selling strategy for every single subscriber
the low price might make him not want to buy instead of buy... keep the content premium priced

palm_comparison.vs_us: No per-fan price discipline or perceived-value rule at Palm (§7 THIN). Direct gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adopt a dynamic per-fan pricing rule keyed to unlock history, plus a floor that keeps explicit content premium-priced to protect perceived value.

new_findings.title: Take a week off at $100k/month to expose founder dependency before it becomes a ceiling

variants.claim: The only reliable way to test whether a $100k/month agency is truly scalable is for the founder to take a full week off and observe whether revenue, creator satisfaction, and employee efficiency hold without them.
variants.what_they_do: Liam prescribes a literal one-week founder absence as a diagnostic: if GMV and creator satisfaction hold or improve, the business is scalable; if revenue drops or creator issues go unhandled, the business is still founder-dependent and specific SOPs are missing.

sources.quote: take a week off and just observe what happens

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm is early-stage with Josh leading acquisition and strategy hands-on (§6). No documented test for founder dependency exists. Given the current scale, this is a forward-looking tool, but the diagnostic framework is immediately useful to plan against.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Schedule a planned 5-day founder-absence drill (even a partial one — Josh steps back from one department at a time) to surface which processes break. Use the results to prioritize which workflows need SOPs or a designated owner first. Document the gaps in Airtable.

new_findings.title: Tie each team role's compensation to the specific business metric that role drives

variants.claim: Every team member should be financially incentivized by the growth of the specific business metric their role controls, so their personal interest and the agency's interest are identical.
variants.what_they_do: TDM structures compensation by role-specific output: media team paid on video performance, marketing team on traffic, chat team on sales closed, recruiting team on hires made. Each person wins when the business wins, but through the metric they personally control — not generic salary.

sources.quote: media team are compensated for these videos doing well. Marketing team are compensated for more traffic. Chat team are compensated for more sales.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's team structure is thin (§10): Amin is a contractor, editors exist, and an external chat team is outsourced. No documented incentive structure or performance-linked comp bands exist. This is a GAP as Palm begins building in-house teams.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When hiring the first in-house chatters (§8 roadmap) and any additional editors or marketing staff, design role-specific KPIs with a variable comp component tied to those KPIs (e.g., chatters earn a bonus % of PPV revenue above a baseline; editors earn a bonus per post that hits a reach threshold). Document comp bands in the org structure build-out.

new_findings.topic: Incremental hiring in step with revenue milestones
new_findings.title: Add team members and build systems in proportion to revenue growth — not all at once

variants.claim: Agencies that scale successfully add team members and formalize systems incrementally as revenue grows (every $10k step), rather than brute-forcing revenue and attempting to retrofit structure later.
variants.what_they_do: TDM describes a stepwise pattern: $10k → $20k adds first team members; $20k → $30k formalizes contracts; $30k → $40k introduces KPI tracking; $40k → $50k and onward continues compounding systems. The key is that structure is built in proportion to the revenue step, not deferred.

sources.quote: 10 turned into 20 but a couple team members came on board. 20 turned into 30 which meant that you started to build out proper contracts.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring and org structure are thin and undocumented (§10 GAP). There is no scaling roadmap that ties headcount or system builds to specific revenue milestones. Palm risks the 'brute force' trap the video warns against if growth outpaces internal structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a simple revenue-milestone roadmap in Airtable or Notion: at each $X increment in monthly revenue, define the next hire, SOP, or system to be built. Even a rough version (e.g., at $X add a second editor; at $Y formalize creator KPI reporting; at $Z begin in-house chat hiring) provides the incremental discipline TDM describes.

new_findings.topic: Multi-account content rotation — avoiding identical Reels grids
new_findings.title: Rotate which repurposed video goes to which account — never mirror the same grid

variants.claim: Posting identical video sequences across all repurposing accounts is the most common way agencies trigger cluster-bans.
variants.what_they_do: If a creator films 3 videos/day, all 3 go to the main organic account, but each repurposing account receives only ONE of the three — a different one per account. This ensures no two reposting accounts share the same Reels grid sequence.

sources.quote: if you have three different videos per day… you're going to post all three of them on the main account. One of them on the first reposting account, one of them on the second, and one of them on the third

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current scheduling system (§5) routes posts via Publer or Telegram/Amin but is designed for one primary account per creator. There is no documented logic for distributing content across a cluster of repurposing accounts for a single creator.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a distribution-rotation rule in the scheduling layer: for any creator running N repurposing accounts, assign each daily video to a unique subset of those accounts via a round-robin or modular assignment in Airtable/Publer. This prevents grid mirroring and reduces ban risk.

new_findings.topic: Organic vs. repurposed account architecture — strict separation
new_findings.title: Never mix organic and repurposed content on the same IG account

variants.claim: Mixing original and repurposed content on one account cancels out the benefits of organic reach while still incurring the algorithmic penalty of repurposed content.
variants.what_they_do: They operate two structurally separate account tiers: (1) main organic accounts where the creator films fresh content every time — these pull the highest views; (2) repurposing-only accounts staffed by VAs, purely for volume. The two tiers are never merged.

sources.quote: you split up into both teams because you have your main chick and you have your side chicks… Never mix the two. Never.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's pipeline (§1) is entirely organic/creator-filmed for the primary account. Palm does not currently run a repurposing-account layer. This finding clarifies the correct architecture IF Palm adds one: keep the existing organic pipeline untouched and stand up a separate repurposing operation alongside it.
palm_comparison.recommendation: If Palm scales to multi-account reposting, codify the two-tier rule in the SMM hub redesign (§11): tag accounts as 'organic-primary' or 'repurpose-only' in Airtable, and enforce via posting-workflow routing so editors never schedule repurposed content to an organic-primary account.

new_findings.topic: VA-staffed repurposing operation — scaling social output without creator time
new_findings.title: Staff a separate VA team to run repurposing accounts, freeing creators for original filming

variants.claim: Repurposing accounts are an operational task fully separable from the creator, enabling unlimited horizontal account scaling via VAs without increasing creator workload.
variants.what_they_do: Once repurposing accounts are set up (distinct identities, rotation rules), the agency hires VAs whose sole job is to download, structurally re-edit, and post content to the repurposing account cluster. The creator only films for the organic primary accounts. This decouples content volume from creator availability.

sources.quote: you can still have your main accounts, your main organic accounts, pull all the views, but you can hire 50 VAs and repost on as many accounts as you like on this side

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently uses Amin (contractor) for manual posting (§5) and editors for production (§1). There is no VA layer for repurposing account management. Hiring/delegation infrastructure for this role does not exist yet (§10 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm is ready to scale social distribution, define a 'Repurposing VA' role with a clear SOP: download source, apply structural edits per checklist, assign to rotation slot, schedule via Publer. Start with one VA per 5 repurposing accounts. Document the SOP before hiring.

new_findings.title: Start with exactly 2 clients — not 1, not 3+

variants.claim: New agencies should aim to sign two clients simultaneously: one as a hedge if the other underperforms, but no more than two to avoid operational overwhelm before systems are proven.
variants.what_they_do: Prescribes a deliberate two-client start: if one relationship fails the other provides continuity, while capping at two prevents the operator from over-extending before they have SOPs, chatters, or reliable traffic in place.

sources.quote: you should ideally aim to sign two clients from the get-go. If you just have one, you can stay with one, of course, but in most cases, one of them might not work out

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented guidance on optimal portfolio size at different stages of agency maturity (§6 GAP). No scaling model or client-count-to-capacity ratio is written down.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define explicit capacity tiers in Palm's onboarding/ops documentation: e.g., Tier 0 (founding) = 1-2 creators, Tier 1 = up to 5 once chatting is delegated, Tier 2 = 10+ once a full team is in place. Use this to gate new creator signings against current team bandwidth.

new_findings.title: Hire chatters on flat rate, commission, or hybrid — reinvest first revenue

variants.claim: Once initial revenue is generated, the first reinvestment should be hiring chatters on a flat rate, small commission, or hybrid comp structure to free up operator time for scaling.
variants.what_they_do: Describes a reinvestment-first scaling loop: operator chats manually → earns first revenue → hires chatters (flat fee or commission or both) → reclaims time → signs more clients → hires more chatters → repeats. The comp structure is flexible (flat/commission/hybrid) rather than a fixed model.

sources.quote: you can then reinvest it and you can hire what are called chatters. These are people who handle all of the DMs for you, either for a flat rate or a small commission or a mixture of both.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) so this loop is bypassed at the current stage. However, Palm has no documented chatter comp model, hiring criteria, or onboarding process for when it builds an in-house team. This is a build-toward gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Begin documenting chatter comp benchmarks now (flat rate ranges, commission % norms, hybrid structures) sourced from competitor content like this. Build a chatter job description and scoring rubric ahead of the in-house chat team launch. This prevents scrambling when the timeline moves up.

new_findings.title: Package VA chatter training as a zipped module with persona PDF

variants.claim: Sending a structured, zipped training module to new chatter VAs — including a persona guide, payment info, and scripts — accelerates onboarding and ensures consistency.
variants.what_they_do: When hiring a chatter VA, Bjorn compiles a ZIP package containing: (1) a PDF guide on the model's persona (age range, niche, typing style, pet names, emojis), (2) payment/platform instructions, (3) a pre-written script. The VA unzips and self-trains before starting.

sources.quote: some training material that I prepare for our VAs so when I hire a VA for the purposes of chatting I'll input the necessary information from the model in this guide here zip it up along with a few other things and then send it to the VA the chatter as a training module

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting to an external team (§8) with no documented internal SOP or onboarding material. There is no equivalent persona brief or chatter training kit. This is a direct gap to fill as Palm builds toward an in-house chat team.
palm_comparison.recommendation: As Palm builds its in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap), create a per-creator ZIP/Notion training module template: persona card (age, niche, tone, emojis, pet names), platform walkthrough, PPV pricing ladder, and a baseline script. Standardize this as the chatter onboarding artifact.

new_findings.title: Start subscribers on $15–$20 PPVs before escalating to $80–$100+

variants.claim: Sending low-ticket PPVs ($15–$20) first gauges a subscriber's willingness to spend and builds purchasing habit before high-ticket ($80–$100+) asks.
variants.what_they_do: Chatter begins by offering content in the $15–$20 range ('dipping a toe in the water'). After multiple purchases at this level, the chatter reads the subscriber's comfort and escalates to high-ticket PPVs ($80, $90, $100+). No high-ticket offer is sent cold to a new subscriber.

sources.quote: you'll start off selling subscriber content from $15 to $20 so it's kind of like dipping into the water seeing where they're at and then after the subscribers purchase multiple pieces you can start selling PPVs for high ticket prices like 80 bucks 90 100

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented PPV pricing ladder or sequencing rules (§7 GAP, §8 outsourced). The external chat team may do this implicitly, but it is not a formalized Palm playbook. This is core build-toward intelligence for the in-house chat roadmap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a 3-tier PPV ladder in the chatter SOP: Tier 1 (entry, $15–$20) → Tier 2 (mid, $30–$50) → Tier 3 (high-ticket, $80–$100+). Require at least 2 Tier 1 purchases before a Tier 3 offer. Encode this sequencing into AI chatting logic when that project advances.

new_findings.title: Send a single sad-face emoji to re-engage non-responding subscribers

variants.claim: Sending a lone sad-face emoji to a subscriber who has gone quiet triggers guilt and reliably provokes a response, re-opening the monetization window.
variants.what_they_do: When a subscriber has not replied to a previous message, instead of sending follow-up texts or more content pitches, the chatter sends only a '😢' or '🥺' emoji. Bjorn claims ~99.9% response rate. The mechanism: guilt-trips the subscriber into explaining their absence, which restarts rapport and spending. No spamming — one emoji only.

sources.quote: if a subscriber has not replied to your previous message and you want to get his attention simply just send a sad face emoji it is amazing how this will work I would say this probably works 99.9% of the time

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented re-engagement tactic (§8 outsourced, §9 partial fan retention). This is a high-signal, zero-cost tactic for the in-house chat roadmap and is also relevant to AI chatting logic (trigger sad emoji after N hours of non-response).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build this into the chatter re-engagement SOP: if subscriber has not replied after 24–48 hours, send a single sad-face emoji (no text). Log the response rate in Fan Tracker CRM to validate. For AI chatting, add as an automated trigger: no-reply after configurable timeout → send sad emoji → await response before any further action.

new_findings.title: Never send multiple follow-up messages to a non-responding subscriber

variants.claim: Sending multiple unanswered messages in a row appears overwhelming and spammy, actively pushing subscribers away and reducing future engagement.
variants.what_they_do: The VA training guide explicitly prohibits sending additional messages if a subscriber has not responded. One message sits unanswered → chatter holds. The only permitted follow-up is the sad-face emoji tactic (single message). This is a firm SOP rule, not a suggestion.

sources.quote: do not spam subscribers with messages if they're not responding try not to send additional messages if the sub is not responding as this can appear overwhelming and spammy and could potentially put the subscriber off

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no internal chat SOP (§8 outsourced). If the external team lacks this discipline it may be causing churn Palm can't see. This rule should be a core constraint in both the in-house chatter SOP and AI chatting prompt guardrails.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a hard rule to the chatter SOP and AI chatting system prompt: after one unanswered message, no further text messages until the subscriber replies (only the sad-face emoji exception is permitted). Make this a QA audit criterion when reviewing chatter logs.

new_findings.title: Daily end-of-shift written takeaways lock in incremental chatter learning

variants.claim: Requiring chatters to record written takeaways at the end of every shift — specifically one actionable improvement for the next shift — accelerates skill development during the first month.
variants.what_they_do: For roughly the first month, each chatter writes a brief end-of-shift note answering: 'What are the actionable steps I'll take next shift to improve on what I did this shift?' This creates a self-directed feedback loop between supervised live-training sessions.

sources.quote: For the first month or so, get the chatters to record their daily takeaways. At the end of each shift, okay, what are the actionable steps you're going to take next shift

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter development loop at Palm (§8, outsourced). Build-toward.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Include a daily takeaway template in the chatter onboarding kit. A simple Airtable or Notion form ('shift date / one thing to improve / how I'll do it differently') submitted at shift-end gives managers a lightweight pulse without requiring constant live supervision.

new_findings.title: Start at 50/50 split as baseline; negotiate higher % as skills improve

variants.claim: 50/50 is the standard baseline commission split for new OFM agencies, with room to take a higher percentage as expertise and results grow.
variants.what_they_do: Agency locks in 50/50 as the minimum split with every creator. As the agency proves its value and techniques mature, it renegotiates upward. No tiered structure described — purely experience-based progression.

sources.quote: you say let's partner up, I'll take 50%, you take 50%, this is the benchmark, this is the baseline

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % per-creator at onboarding (§7) but has no documented split rationale, tiers, or progression logic. The baseline number Palm uses is not written down. This is flagged as §7's #1 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify 50/50 as Palm's stated baseline in the onboarding contract and pitch deck. Document criteria for when Palm negotiates above 50% (e.g., creator already earning >$X/mo, Palm providing full chatting, paid traffic spend). Gives sales clarity and creator trust.

new_findings.title: Target creators who are plateaued, overwhelmed, or burned by a bad agency

variants.claim: The highest-converting creator prospects are those who are stuck at a plateau, overwhelmed by workload, or have had a bad agency experience — not cold beginners.
variants.what_they_do: Agency explicitly looks for creators who: (a) plateaued after initial growth, (b) are overwhelmed managing content + chatting alone, (c) had a bad prior agency and are open to a better partner. Uses these pain points as the outreach hook.

sources.quote: you're looking for models that are a bit overwhelmed, they're a bit stuck, their accounts are being plateaued, maybe they've had a bad agency before

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented ICP (ideal creator profile) or qualification criteria for outbound (§6). Josh-led DM outreach is ad hoc. Targeting burned/plateaued creators is a concrete, testable filter Palm could apply immediately.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define Palm's creator ICP in writing: earning $1K–$10K/mo, plateaued 60+ days, active IG but not growing, or recently left an agency. Use these as DM targeting filters on IG/TikTok. Add a qualification question to the onboarding form: 'Have you worked with an agency before? What happened?'

new_findings.title: Run weekly payouts and performance check-ins as a retention and motivation tool

variants.claim: Weekly payouts ('weekly pouch') keep both the agency and creator motivated and signal operational professionalism early in the relationship.
variants.what_they_do: After onboarding, agency moves to a weekly payout cadence. Framed as both a financial rhythm and a morale tool — seeing money arrive weekly reinforces creator buy-in and agency credibility. No detailed reporting format described, but the weekly cadence is presented as standard practice.

sources.quote: after a week or two, we do weekly pouch, just to let you know — that's just a cool little tip, enjoy the money coming in

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm auto-computes weekly invoices (§7) so the cadence matches. However, Palm has no documented creator-facing performance review or check-in tied to the payout — it's finance-only. No retention/success cadence is written down (§9 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Attach a lightweight weekly creator report to Palm's existing invoice email: top 3 metrics (subscribers, revenue, top-performing post). Takes minimal effort given Palm's Airtable data and Resend pipeline, and transforms a finance touchpoint into a retention and upsell moment.

new_findings.title: Hierarchical folder labeling system for OF vault content reuse

variants.claim: Organizing vault content by year/quarter/month/week with descriptive labels enables fast retrieval and reselling to new subscribers.
variants.what_they_do: Creator maintains a Dropbox folder tree (Year → Q1-Q4 → Month → Week → Day), names individual files descriptively (e.g. 'pink outfit 1', 'pink outfit 2'), and mirrors this structure inside the OF vault using OF's native label/folder feature. Only edited, reusable content is saved. Legacy content is batched into a single 'pre-2022' folder and backfilled gradually rather than all at once.

sources.quote: I took everything from before 2022 and I put that in one folder and now I created a new folder and it is called of content 2023 and from there I have q1 2 3 and 4

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content system (§2) is THIN on OF vault/PPV organization. The social pipeline (§1) is mature for top-of-funnel assets but there is no documented system for tagging, retrieving, or reselling vault content. This is a direct gap in the OF vault layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Define a vault taxonomy standard for Palm-managed creators: Year → Month → content-type label (e.g. '2024-03-booty', '2024-03-face'). Add a vault-labeling step to the existing editor workflow so every uploaded asset is tagged before the task is closed. Build a lightweight 'resell menu' Airtable view that surfaces labeled vault assets by creator for chatter/PPV use.

new_findings.title: Pin a pre-made content menu to OF page to drive passive vault resales

variants.claim: Pinning a priced menu of previously sent content to the OF page lets new subscribers self-serve old vault content without creator intervention.
variants.what_they_do: Creator builds a text-based menu listing past content items with descriptions and prices, pins it to their OF page. New subscribers browse it and request items by name; creator pulls from the vault folder and sends. No active selling required — the menu does the work.

sources.quote: I also created another menu that has previously sent out content so I've just organized it I label what it is and the price and then I've also pinned that to my page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented equivalent for a passive vault-resell menu (§2 gap). The chatting function is outsourced (§8), so the external chat team likely has no visibility into a structured content menu either. This is a low-effort monetization layer currently missing.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Standardize a pinned content menu for each Palm creator. The editor or ops team maintains it in Airtable (listing vault item name, label, price, thumbnail). Brief the external chat team to reference this menu when fans ask about past content. When the in-house chat team is built (~1yr roadmap), make menu-referencing a core chat SOP.

new_findings.title: Send at least one PPV mass message per day — treat it like TV advertising repetition

variants.claim: Sending PPV messages at minimum once daily, using a TV-advertising repetition mindset, is the single biggest lever for DM revenue most creators underuse.
variants.what_they_do: Creator sends PPV mass messages at least once per day, sometimes multiple times. Frames it explicitly as a repetition/awareness play (Tide detergent analogy): fans who don't buy message #1 may buy message #5. Explicitly rejects the 'one message per week' approach. Pairs this with multiple free posts per day on the OF feed to maintain presence.

sources.quote: you should be sending out pay-per-view messages at least once a day if you're not you are probably missing out on a lot of money

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is outsourced (§8) and there is no documented PPV cadence SOP. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and the AI chatting project. The daily-minimum rule is a concrete, codifiable KPI.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a PPV send-frequency KPI to the external chat team's performance brief immediately: minimum 1 mass PPV per creator per day. When building the in-house chat SOP (roadmap), hard-code this as a daily task in the chatter checklist. For the AI chatting project, model automated PPV scheduling at 1× daily with A/B price variation.

new_findings.topic: subscriber list segmentation for PPV targeting
new_findings.title: Segment subscriber lists by content preference and renewal status to send targeted PPV messages

variants.claim: Using OF's native list feature to segment fans by content preference (feet, striptease, etc.) and renewal status enables personalized PPV sends that feel direct and increase conversion.
variants.what_they_do: Creates named lists inside OF (beyond defaults): VIP list for high-paying subscribers ($200/mo tier who get PPV free), preference-based lists (feet fans, striptease fans, etc.). When sending a PPV mass message, selects the relevant list as the target or excludes VIP list from paid messages. Sends preference-matched messages to niche lists with personalized copy to increase perceived intimacy.

sources.quote: you can also create lists for certain people like if you know there are certain people that really like feet pictures or there's certain group of people that really like striptease videos you can add them to that list and then whenever you do want to send out a certain type of message you can send it to everybody

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's fan intelligence layer (§9) has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale-hunting module but no documented PPV targeting by content preference. Chatting is outsourced (§8) so the external team may not be segmenting sends. This is build-toward for both the in-house chat team and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Brief the external chat team to begin using OF list segmentation immediately: at minimum, maintain a 'VIP/renewing' list excluded from paid PPV, and build 2–3 preference lists per creator based on DM history. Feed preference data back into the Fan Tracker CRM (§9). When building the in-house chat SOP, make list maintenance a weekly chatter task. For the AI chatting project, model preference tagging as a fan-profile attribute driving automated PPV selection.

new_findings.title: Golden Ratio: 60 DMs/day for 30 days guarantees first client

variants.claim: Sending 60 outreach DMs per day (10/hour, 6 hours/day, 7 days/week) produces 1,680 messages per month, which is sufficient to sign at least one client.
variants.what_they_do: Agency instructs new operators to hit exactly 60 DMs per day split into 6 one-hour blocks. At that pace, 1,680 monthly contacts makes signing a single client near-certain. They also recommend tracking every outreach in a spreadsheet and following up with non-responders.

sources.quote: For 30 days, if you do six 60 outreach messages a day, you can do 10 per hour, six times a day

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no codified daily volume target or tracking cadence (§6). No 'golden ratio' equivalent exists internally — outreach volume is ad hoc.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Codify a minimum daily outreach quota (e.g., 60 DMs/day) for whoever owns acquisition at Palm. Build or adopt a simple lead tracker (Airtable view or spreadsheet) to log status and follow-ups, matching the systematic volume this competitor prescribes.

new_findings.title: Cross-platform username matching: find OF creators on X, then locate on IG

variants.claim: Creators typically use the same username on X/Twitter and Instagram, so you can search 'only fans' on X, collect usernames, then find and DM the same accounts on Instagram.
variants.what_they_do: Operator searches the word 'only fans' on X to surface creator accounts, copies their username, pastes it into Instagram search, and DMs there. They also use Instagram's 'related accounts' graph — following lists, likers, and commenters of one known OF creator — to find unlimited additional prospects.

sources.quote: you simply go on X and you search the word only fans on the search bar... Go on to Instagram and copy and paste the same username

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented prospect-sourcing method beyond Josh's personal DMs and referrals (§6). No cross-platform lookup or IG graph-traversal tactic is codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document a prospect-sourcing SOP using both methods: (1) X keyword search → IG username lookup, and (2) IG related-accounts traversal from any known OF creator. This is low-cost and immediately actionable for whoever runs outreach at Palm.

new_findings.title: First DM: short curiosity-piquing message focused on a specific observable problem

variants.claim: The opening DM should be short, reference one specific problem visible on the creator's profile (e.g., wrong link-in-bio, no reels), and pique curiosity — never a long copy-paste paragraph.
variants.what_they_do: Agents identify a concrete, observable weakness on the creator's profile before messaging, then write a brief DM that names that problem implicitly or asks a curiosity-driving question. No feature-dumping or price-quoting in the first message. Outreach is sent from a personal IG account even with as few as 150 followers.

sources.quote: focus on the problem that specific model has. So this could be the lack of posting reels, the wrong link in bio

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented outreach message framework or script (§6 GAP). First-contact approach is entirely at Josh's discretion.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a first-DM template library: short (1–3 lines), profile-specific observation + curiosity hook, no agency pitch. Test from a personal or agency IG account. This is a direct complement to Palm's existing pipeline — once a creator is signed the content engine (§1) kicks in immediately.

new_findings.title: Google Drive onboarding folder: info sheet + logins + pre-made content scripts

variants.claim: A structured onboarding folder with three components — model info sheet, platform logins, and pre-made PPV content scripts — prevents miscommunication and enables the chatting team to start immediately.
variants.what_they_do: On signing, the agency shares a Google Drive folder. The model info sheet captures personal details (favorite color, food, pet names, etc.) that chatters need to maintain consistent persona in DMs. Logins for all managed platforms are collected at once. Pre-made scripts (photo/video sequences already shot) are also uploaded so chatters can sell PPV from day one. Expectations are set in writing: communication only via WhatsApp group, Monday–Friday 9–5 local time.

sources.quote: within our Google Drive folder, we have three things. the model information sheets... the login... and then last but not least is the scripts

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's onboarding collects name, email, commission %, state, contract, and voice memo via a form wizard (§6), but has no documented model info sheet for chatters, no platform-login collection protocol, and no pre-made content script intake. Communication boundary-setting is not formalized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add three items to Palm's onboarding wizard or a linked Google Drive template: (1) a model info sheet with personal details for the chatting team, (2) a platform-login collection step, (3) a pre-made content upload prompt. Also add a written communication expectations section (channel, hours) to the onboarding flow to reduce creator churn friction.

new_findings.title: Escalating PPV price ladder: free photo → $15 → $50 → $100 → $150 → $200

variants.claim: Sending a free teaser photo followed by a scripted sequence of PPV content with escalating price points ($15, $50, $100, $150, $200) converts fans into high spenders by building investment and desire incrementally.
variants.what_they_do: Chatters follow a pre-built content flow the creator has shot in advance. Conversation starts organically, then a free clothed photo is sent. First PPV is priced low ($15–20, ~15–20 second video). Free content interspersed between paid unlocks to maintain engagement. Each subsequent PPV increases in price and explicitness. A single fan session can yield $300–400. Chatters run 24/7 in 8-hour rotating shifts (3 chatters per creator account), sourced primarily from South Africa for native English and cultural fluency.

sources.quote: the price goes from $15 to 50, then to 100, then to $150, then to $200, and before you know it, the person has spent maybe $300, $400

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's chatting is fully outsourced (§8) with no internal SOP, scripts, or pricing ladder documented. This is build-toward intelligence for both the in-house chat team roadmap and the AI chatting project.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Capture this escalating PPV ladder as the foundational script template for Palm's future in-house chat team. Specifically: define free-teaser → low-anchor PPV → mid-tier PPV → high-tier PPV price points; have creators pre-shoot content sequences in order before chatters launch; use South Africa as primary chatter sourcing market for English-speaking creator accounts. Brief the external chat team on whether they already use this structure.

new_findings.title: Do chatting yourself for 2 weeks before hiring, then hire South African chatters for cultural fluency

variants.claim: Agency owners should personally do DM chatting for two weeks to understand fan psychology and upsell mechanics before hiring chatters, and should prioritize South African hires over Southeast Asian for native English slang and cultural alignment.
variants.what_they_do: New agency operators personally handle all DMs for roughly two weeks to learn what works. They then hire chatters from South Africa (preferred) or South America, paying in local currency (favorable exchange rate). Three chatters per creator account rotate 8-hour shifts for 24/7 coverage. Chatters are trained by the operator who now understands the product firsthand.

sources.quote: step two, is to hire chatters... the vast majority of our team is from SA, from South Africa. Mainly because English is their first language

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting entirely today (§8); this tactic is build-toward for the in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap). No internal SOP exists for chatter hiring geography or shift structure.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When Palm begins building its in-house chat team, use this framework: (1) have the team lead personally chat for 2 weeks per creator to develop training materials, (2) prioritize South African candidates for English-language creator accounts, (3) structure 3-chatter rotating 8-hour shifts per active creator account. Document this in the chat-team hiring SOP before recruitment begins.

new_findings.title: Never sell PPV packs or bundles — sell individual pieces only

variants.claim: Selling content in packs commoditizes it and undermines the per-item price anchoring that drives high revenue per fan.
variants.what_they_do: The agency explicitly bans 'packs' as a pricing format. All PPV is sold as individual items at or above the price floors. The reasoning is that packs reframe the value proposition toward volume/discount, which is the same psychological trap as low per-item pricing.

sources.quote: you especially don't want to sell packs no packs

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented stance on packs vs. individual PPV pricing (§7 GAP). No internal policy exists.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'no packs' rule to the Palm pricing policy alongside the price floors. Instruct the external chat team accordingly. When building the in-house chat SOP, codify individual-item-only PPV selling as a default.

new_findings.topic: AI chatbot displacement of human chatters
new_findings.title: AI chatbots will soon displace low-cost human chatters — build AI chat now

variants.claim: AI chatbots are improving rapidly and will soon deliver low-latency, high-quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest human VAs, making human chatting teams economically unviable.
variants.what_they_do: The consultant flags AI chat as an imminent market force that will undercut human chatters (including cheap Filipino VA teams) on both cost and latency, framing it as a structural shift rather than a distant future trend.

sources.quote: AI chat bots are making huge improvements very quickly and will soon become a dominant force in the market, delivering low latency, high quality chat at a fraction of the cost of even the cheapest of Filipino virtual assistants

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's roadmap already includes an AI chatting project (§8), which directly anticipates this shift. This finding is high-confidence validation that Palm's AI chat roadmap is correctly timed and should be accelerated rather than deprioritized.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use this as justification to prioritize the AI chatting roadmap project. When evaluating build vs. buy, note that the window to build a proprietary advantage is narrowing as third-party AI chat vendors improve. Capture competitor AI chat implementations (scripts, fan-tier logic, PPV sequencing) as build-toward intelligence per §8.

new_findings.title: Multi-stage hiring funnel with hard filters before any offer

variants.claim: A slow, multi-stage hiring funnel with strict filtering criteria is cheaper than hiring the wrong person, whose true cost in lost time, training, and mistakes runs into tens of thousands of dollars.
variants.what_they_do: Agency runs a dedicated hiring funnel lasting roughly one week: job post → multiple filtering stages → skills/personality tests → pick only the single best-qualified applicant. If no applicant clears the bar, the owner does the job themselves for another 2 weeks and re-runs the funnel rather than settling. Once hired, the employee gets a fully thought-through onboarding, individual KPI tracking, and formal performance reviews.

sources.quote: set up a direct hiring funnel that may even take like a week to get all the applicants

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's hiring is not formally documented (§10 — THIN). There is no written hiring funnel, scorecard, or onboarding SOP. Josh currently drives acquisition and ops largely himself, which matches the 'doing it yourself while you find the right person' fallback, but the structured multi-stage filter does not exist.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design a formal 2–3 stage hiring funnel for each role Palm needs next (e.g., editor, chatter, VA): job post → async task/test → live screen → offer. Define a written 'must-have' scorecard per role before opening any search. Document the cost-of-bad-hire math internally so the team doesn't rush to fill seats.

new_findings.title: Structured 5-step onboarding checklist for new creator sign-ons

variants.claim: A repeatable onboarding sequence — contract → account access → OF account audit → marketing setup → expectations meeting — prevents early relationship failures.
variants.what_they_do: After contract signing (via PandaDoc or DocuSign), agency logs into creator's OF account (coordinating 2FA in real time), audits and organizes the subscriber list by spend tier, organizes the content vault, sets up missing social platforms (IG, TikTok, Twitter), sends a content schedule, and holds an expectations-setting conversation covering response windows, platform norms, and month-1/2/3 milestones.

sources.quote: first step is to of course do the onboarding right so the onboarding entails a couple of different things

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a form-wizard onboarding that captures name, email, commission %, state, contract, and voice memo (§6), but no documented post-signing operational checklist covering OF account audit, vault organization, social setup, or expectations management. The onboarding stops at the contract layer.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend Palm's onboarding wizard with a post-contract ops checklist: (1) OF login + 2FA coordination, (2) subscriber spend-sort, (3) vault audit/organization, (4) social platform gap-fill, (5) content schedule delivery, (6) expectations call with month-1/2/3 benchmarks. Build this as a checklist state-machine in the Creators table or a dedicated onboarding UI.

new_findings.title: Use US SIM / US-based poster for non-English-speaking creators to avoid low-spend audiences

variants.claim: If a creator posts TikTok from a country with weak purchasing power (e.g., Colombia), viral reach fills her funnel with followers who cannot afford OF subscriptions — a US SIM card or US proxy poster fixes the audience geography.
variants.what_they_do: For creators based in Latin America or other non-Tier-1 countries, agency either ships/provides a US SIM card so the creator's device registers as US-based, or recruits a US-based person to post on her behalf, ensuring TikTok's algorithm surfaces content to US audiences.

sources.quote: you would not really my advice would not be for you to get her to post it herself on Tik Tok because you'll if she goes viral she'll get a bunch of Colombian followers and the currency exchange doesn't really make sense

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a hardware-isolated posting system for AI accounts (§3/§4) with dedicated SIMs per persona, but no documented geo-routing strategy for real creators posting from non-Tier-1 countries. This gap could be costing subscriber conversion rates for any internationally-based Palm creators. (§5 gap)
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit Palm's current real-creator roster for location. For any creator outside Tier-1 English-speaking markets, evaluate whether their TikTok posts are geo-targeting US/UK audiences. If not, explore US SIM provisioning using the existing SIM Inventory infrastructure (§3) or route their posts through Amin with a US-based device.

new_findings.topic: Pre-loaded chat scripts with embedded media
new_findings.title: Equip chatters with pre-written scripts that have photos/videos pre-attached for real-time sends

variants.claim: Chatting drives 80%+ of OF revenue; chatters must have scripts with specific photos, videos, and voice notes pre-loaded so they can send PPV content in real time without hunting the vault mid-conversation.
variants.what_they_do: Agency builds a script library where each conversation branch has the relevant media asset already tagged/attached. Chatters follow the script and send the pre-matched content without interruption, maintaining flow and urgency in the conversation.

sources.quote: 80% or more should come from the chatting the messages and the tips so because that's the most important part you must have a system in place so you must have set scripts that you or if you've hired somebody to the to do the chatting they can follow now Within These scripts they should have pre-made content already there so photos and videos maybe even some voice notes

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm currently outsources chatting (§8) and has no internal SOP or script library. This is directly relevant build-toward intelligence for Palm's planned in-house chat team (~1yr roadmap) and AI chatting project. The key structural insight is media-to-script pre-linking, not just having scripts alone.
palm_comparison.recommendation: When building Palm's in-house chat system, design the script library with media assets pre-linked at the node level (not stored separately). Each chat script node should reference a specific Asset ID from the content vault. This prevents chatters from breaking conversation flow to search the vault — a documented friction point in competitor agencies.

new_findings.topic: Reddit subreddit and content testing cadence
new_findings.title: Test subreddits by upvote threshold; swap non-performers; prioritise content quality above all

variants.claim: Content quality is the single most important Reddit variable — good content can overcome mediocre subreddits, but bad content fails even in perfect subreddits.
variants.what_they_do: Start with a niche (e.g. feet), find subreddits by searching and crawling competitor model pages. Post in each; if a subreddit doesn't reach ~20+ upvotes, swap it for a new one. Simultaneously test content by finding top-performing posts within target subreddits and asking the creator to recreate them with a personal twist. Scale to 20 subreddits/account then plateau for a month before expanding further.

sources.quote: we basically test the subreddit… if it doesn't get like 20 plus uploads a day then we just change them out for new ones

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content ideation engine (§1) is strong for social reels but is not applied to Reddit content selection. No subreddit testing framework exists (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Adapt Palm's existing inspo-scrape pipeline (§1) to pull top Reddit posts from target subreddits as content briefs — same viral-score logic, different surface. Add subreddit performance tracking (upvote rate per post) to Airtable so low-performing subs are rotated out systematically.

new_findings.title: Model Reddit captions on top-performing posts in target subreddit, then iterate own voice

variants.claim: Beginners should copy the structure and tone of high-performing captions in their target subreddits (not verbatim) and only develop original voice after accumulating posting experience.
variants.what_they_do: Find the most-upvoted posts in the subreddits being targeted. Extract caption patterns (length, phrasing style, call-to-action type). Write similar-but-not-identical captions. After sufficient volume, the operator develops intuition for original captions. Treat caption testing as iterative — same test-and-replace logic applied to subreddits and content.

sources.quote: we take inspiration a lot of the time from bigger models that are doing well in the subre we post in… make it similar but not the exact same

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's inspo pipeline (§1) extracts hooks and captions from viral IG reels for social content. The same intelligence layer does not exist for Reddit. No Reddit caption SOP documented (§5 🔲 GAP).
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend the Airtable content pipeline to include a Reddit Caption Bank: a table of top-performing captions per subreddit, refreshed weekly, used as briefs for whoever manages the Reddit accounts. This reuses the same inspo-scrape logic Palm already has — just pointed at Reddit instead of Instagram.

new_findings.title: Use carousels as growth drivers, text posts as volume filler on Threads

variants.claim: Carousel posts move the needle most on Threads, but text/comment posts are needed as cheap filler to hit daily volume targets.
variants.what_they_do: TDM designates carousel posts as their primary growth format ('goats') and text/comment posts as filler. Of the 5–8 daily posts, at least a couple are carousels; the rest are short text posts. They prioritise content that is shareable and/or controversial as the single biggest lever.

sources.quote: comment posts as your fillers and then carousel posts as the goats. These are the kind of things that are really going to move the needle with your page

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's content pipeline (§1) is built around IG Reels and carousels but is not mapped to Threads formats. The inspo-driven pipeline could surface shareable content concepts that apply cross-platform, but there is no Threads-specific content classification.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Extend the content pipeline's format taxonomy to include 'Threads carousel' and 'Threads text post' types. When the AI vision analysis (Step 3) rates a reel high on shareability/controversy, flag it as a Threads carousel candidate as well.

new_findings.title: Salary creator model: pay creator fixed rate, agency keeps ~90% of revenue

variants.claim: In a vertical-scale agency, paying creators a fixed monthly salary (e.g. $2,500–$10,000) regardless of page revenue allows the agency to retain ~90% of profits.
variants.what_they_do: Agency signs creators on a flat monthly salary tied to cost-of-living (e.g. $2,500–$10,000/mo depending on location). If the page earns $100K/mo, the agency keeps ~$90K. Creator has no performance incentive; agency owns all marketing decisions. This is contrasted with rev-share models where creator keeps 70%+ and agency takes ~30%.

sources.quote: I could theoretically be paying this creator $2500 every single month, depending on where she lives, upwards to $10,000 a month. I keep 90% of the profit.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm uses a commission/revenue-split model set per creator at onboarding (§7) — no documented salary-creator option. Palm's split rationale is not written down (§7 GAP). The salary model is a materially different deal structure Palm has not codified.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a 'salary creator' tier to Palm's deal structure documentation (§7). Define eligibility criteria (e.g. creator has no existing following; Palm does all marketing) and salary bands by geography. This model makes most sense for Palm's AI personas or for raw-talent real creators with no existing audience, where Palm controls 100% of the funnel.

new_findings.topic: Tiered commission escalation to reduce signing friction
new_findings.title: Start at 20-30% commission and escalate by milestone to earn the full split

variants.claim: New agencies without proven results should offer a tiered commission structure — low percentage at signing, escalating to 50% only after hitting defined revenue milestones — to reduce creator risk and incentivize agency performance.
variants.what_they_do: One speaker describes his first deal: 30% upfront, moving to 40% at $15k/month, then 50/50 above $30k. The logic is explicit: 'I didn't know anything about this game' and going in at 50% without proof is unjustifiable for the creator. The escalator also creates aligned incentives — the agency is motivated to grow the account because their own take increases. A complementary tactic: for established creators already making (e.g.) $5k/month, guarantee that floor no matter what the agency does in month one, demonstrating good faith.

sources.quote: my first Creator I went in at 30% and I said at 30% I will start if we get past 15K it'll move to 40 and if I can get you past 30k we'll go 50/50 from there moving forward

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm sets commission % at onboarding as a fixed field (§7), but there is no documented tiered/milestone-based commission model or floor-guarantee tactic for established creators. No internal baseline exists for how Palm's splits are structured or justified. This is a §7 gap.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Develop a documented commission framework with two tracks: (1) New creator / unproven account — start at 30-35%, escalate to 50% upon hitting defined monthly revenue threshold (suggest $10k/month, consistent with the 3-month target the speakers reference). (2) Established creator already earning — offer a monthly floor guarantee equal to their current earnings; charge full split only on revenue above that floor in month one. Codify both in the onboarding contract wizard.

new_findings.topic: Creator vetting criteria and red-flag screening
new_findings.title: Screen creators for effort-mindset before signing; avoid purely passive-income-seekers

variants.claim: Agencies should qualify creators on mindset — specifically filtering out those who want purely passive income — because they disengage during the experimentation phase and waste agency resources.
variants.what_they_do: The speakers describe two acquisition filters: (1) prefer creators who have already tried OF themselves and seen some traction, because they understand the work involved and appreciate agency value more; (2) screen out creators who articulate 'I just want to make money passively' as their primary motivation, since they won't fulfill content obligations and the agency's marketing investment becomes a gamble. They also describe a 7-step creator vetting process for creators evaluating agencies (social proof → documented process → call → ask deliverable questions → watch for red flags like guaranteed numbers or pressure to sign quickly), which implicitly describes what a credible agency's pitch should contain.

sources.quote: me personally when it comes to new creators I definitely would say I'm kind of looking at the mindset that they're in before coming into this like if they're just in this I just want to make money passively then forget it it's not going to work

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's acquisition is thin (§6) with no documented qualification criteria or creator scoring beyond the onboarding form fields. There is no mindset screen or preference framework for experienced-vs-new creators. The onboarding wizard captures logistics (name, commission, contract) but not fit signals. Palm's 75-convo market research was acquisition discovery, not an ongoing qualification system.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add a creator qualification layer before the onboarding form is sent: a 5-10 minute discovery call or async screening form with 3-4 questions targeting: (1) how long have they been on OF and what's their current monthly range, (2) what are they willing to commit to weekly in content production, (3) what's their primary goal (scale income vs. passive). Define a simple pass/fail criteria. Prioritize creators already making $1-5k/month who understand the platform — the speakers call this the ideal profile for fast results and agency appreciation.

new_findings.title: Segment OF creators into 4 revenue tiers to target the right clients

variants.claim: Agencies should segment potential creator clients into four revenue bands and target the tier where their service delivers the highest ROI.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan segments OF creators into four bands: $0–10K/mo, $10–50K/mo, $100–500K/mo, and $500K+/mo. New/smaller agencies are directed to target the $0–10K band because those creators get the biggest ROI from management services. He sends clients a segmentation sheet and has them identify which band to focus on exclusively, so outreach and pitching is concentrated rather than scattered.

sources.quote: we split there up into four market segments right only fans models making 500k a month only fans models making between 100 500k a month only fans models making 10 to 50K a month and only fans models anything from 0 to 10K

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has no documented targeting criteria for which creator tier to pursue (§6 GAP). Acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no written segmentation model. This competitor has a concrete framework that tells a new agency owner exactly which creators to approach and why.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Build a one-page creator segmentation model for Palm: define the four revenue tiers, articulate the ROI argument for each, and codify which tier(s) Palm currently targets and why. Use this as a qualifier in outbound DM scripts so Josh — and eventually any acquisition hire — is fishing in the right pond from day one.

new_findings.title: Proactively explain 'why' behind every agency decision to prevent creator churn from miscommunication

variants.claim: Most creator-agency relationship problems stem from unclear communication about what the agency does and why, not from the tactics themselves.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's top lesson is that agencies fail creators by not explaining the reasoning behind decisions. When a creator complains or pushes back, the wrong response is 'we just do this because it works.' The right response is to genuinely understand what's bothering them — is it the content type? The social media time demand? The lack of transparency? He builds in active listening and clear explanation as a core operational habit, not an ad-hoc fix.

sources.quote: a lot of issues that are going to arise are going to be from you not communicating clearly with your client on like what you do and just setting expectations framing stuff the correct way

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's creator onboarding captures basics (form wizard, contract, voice memo — §6) but there is no documented ongoing communication cadence or SOP for handling creator objections/concerns. As Palm scales, this gap will compound — creators managed at arm's length without regular 'why' explanations will churn faster.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Add two communication elements to Palm's creator management SOP: (1) An onboarding 'strategy brief' call where Palm explains the content pipeline, posting cadence, and chatting approach — and why each element works — before anything goes live. (2) A recurring creator check-in (bi-weekly or monthly) with a structured agenda: results review, upcoming content plan, anything the creator is uncomfortable with. Template these in Airtable against the `Creators` table.

new_findings.title: Master one marketing channel completely before adding others

variants.claim: Agencies that spread effort across many marketing channels become mediocre at all of them; depth in one channel outperforms breadth across many.
variants.what_they_do: Dylan's second top lesson: pick one traffic/marketing method and become the best at it before expanding. He acknowledges the risk of single-channel dependency (e.g., TikTok declining) but frames transferable skills as the safety net — skills learned on TikTok apply to Instagram Reels or YouTube Shorts. The discipline is still to go deep on one channel first, then adapt skills to adjacent channels when the primary shows diminishing returns.

sources.quote: lesson two is always going to go back to marketing so many people are trying to do everything yeah just focus on one thing and become the best at it

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's social pipeline (§1) is built around Instagram/Reels as the primary channel with some TikTok. The inspo-scraping system is IG-centric. This is aligned with Dylan's advice. However, Palm has no documented decision framework for when to add a new channel or deprecate an underperforming one. The gap is strategic channel governance, not channel selection itself.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Document Palm's primary channel as Instagram Reels (current de facto state) and define explicit criteria for adding a second channel — e.g., 'when the IG pipeline is running at full cadence for 90 days with consistent growth, evaluate Reddit or TikTok as channel two.' This prevents premature channel sprawl and gives the team clear focus. Also document skill-transfer logic: the IG Reels inspo pipeline (§1) is reusable for TikTok and YouTube Shorts — make this explicit so editors understand the transferability.

new_findings.title: Keep OF link off satellite accounts — funnel to intermediate IG pages only

variants.claim: Placing the OF link only on the main creator page (not on satellite/funnel accounts) reduces satellite account suspension rate to approximately 1-in-20.
variants.what_they_do: Satellite accounts never carry any OF or external link in their bios. Their sole job is to drive IG profile visits to the creator's main account (or a set of 5 intermediate accounts). The OF link lives exclusively at the final destination page. This separation keeps satellite accounts off Meta's adult-content radar and dramatically lowers ban rates.

sources.quote: in none of these accounts' bios, we're putting that link... we're just funneling to other Instagram pages, so our accounts can stay way safer

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's current documented setup (§5) routes traffic through a single creator IG → link-in-bio → OF. The satellite layer with link-segregation is not present. Palm's AI persona accounts do use Beacons link-in-bio (§3), which may face similar flagging risk if OF is referenced. This architecture principle — isolate the OF link to as few accounts as possible — is directly applicable.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Audit current Palm creator IG setups: does every account that references OF carry the link? If so, consider a two-tier architecture where traffic-generation accounts funnel to a single 'conversion' account that holds the OF link. This reduces surface area for platform enforcement actions across the entire portfolio.

new_findings.title: Promote top-20% chatters to shift leads who own whale accounts and implement systems first

variants.claim: Top-performing chatters should be elevated to shift leads with higher-value responsibilities — handling whales, receiving best subscribers, implementing new systems, and potentially earning better pay — to incentivize excellence without necessarily moving them to pure management.
variants.what_they_do: Top 20% by leaderboard score become shift leads. Responsibilities: handle all whale interactions, assigned the highest-value subscribers, first cohort to test and implement any new system or script, eligible for increased pay tied to sustained performance. They remain chatters but are the internal standard-setters.

sources.quote: the top 20%, so the best of the best, they become shift leads. They have the responsibility to handle whales

palm_comparison.vs_us: No internal chatter structure exists at Palm (§8). This shift-lead model is a concrete org design to adopt when building the in-house team — it creates a promotion path without pulling best chatters away from revenue-generating work.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Design the in-house chat org with two tiers from the start: chatters and shift leads. Write the shift-lead job description now (whale ownership, system rollout, quality oversight) so the role is clear before the first hire. Tie shift-lead pay to whale revenue metrics, not just tenure.

new_findings.title: Cross-train all chatters on a single universal persona tone, especially for whale handoffs

variants.claim: Whales who experience a persona or tone shift at handoff disengage and churn — every chatter must be trained to voice the same persona regardless of which shift they are on.
variants.what_they_do: All chatters are cross-trained on one universal tone and persona voice. Whale interactions are treated as the highest-risk handoff: if Chatter A builds rapport with a whale and Chatter B takes over sounding different, the whale is lost. They explicitly state they have lost 'a few good whales' from this failure. The fix is a documented tone/persona guide that is part of chatter onboarding and ongoing training.

sources.quote: they all need to be cross-trained and they need to talk in the same persona. There's one universal tone on how to talk to people, especially whales

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm has a whale-hunting and Fan Tracker CRM system (§9) but no internal chatter voice/persona standard (§8, outsourced). For the AI-persona line (§3), Palm already thinks in terms of a consistent synthetic persona — that same discipline needs to carry into the chat layer when brought in-house.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Create a persona voice guide for each creator before the in-house chat team goes live. Define: vocabulary/slang the creator uses, emotional register, how she responds to high spenders vs. new subs, hard boundaries on what she'd 'say.' Make this guide mandatory reading at chatter onboarding. For AI personas (§3), this guide already partially exists — extract and formalize it.

new_findings.title: Charge $1,500–$2,500/month per client to hit $10K–$20K with 4–8 clients

variants.claim: The sweet spot for agency pricing is $1,500–$2,500/month, allowing a solo operator to hit $10K/month with just four clients rather than needing 50+ low-ticket clients.
variants.what_they_do: Targets $1,500–$2,500/month retainers per client. At $2,500 x 4 clients = $10K/month; 8 clients = $20K/month. One person can manage up to 25–26 clients at this tier before needing to hire.

sources.quote: 1,500 to 2,500, which is a nice sweet spot, you can. Because let's do the math here. 4 * 2,500 10K.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's commission structure is % of creator revenue (§7), not a flat retainer — so pricing model differs structurally. However, Palm has no documented rationale for its split percentages or floor minimums (§7 🔲 GAP). The 'fewer high-value clients beats many low-value clients' logic maps directly to Palm's creator portfolio sizing question.
palm_comparison.recommendation: Use the $1,500–$2,500 benchmark as a reference when modeling minimum viable creator revenue per account Palm should onboard. Define a floor: if a creator is unlikely to generate enough revenue to justify Palm's management overhead, don't sign them. Document the commission split rationale explicitly to fill the §7 gap.

new_findings.title: Differentiate from automated competitors by calling clients and treating them as real people

variants.claim: At premium price points, agencies can and should offer direct human communication — calls, responsive emails, real answers — which becomes the actual differentiator against lower-priced automated competitors.
variants.what_they_do: Explicitly offers phone calls, responsive emails, and real conversations with clients as part of service delivery. Frames this as only economically viable at $1,500–$2,500+/month (can't afford it at $97). Positions human accessibility as the competitive moat against SaaS/AI commodity offerings.

sources.quote: we're going to be normal human beings. That's right. We're going to call them. We're going to send them emails. We're going to answer their questions.

palm_comparison.vs_us: Palm's creator relationships are managed by Josh (acquisition/strategy) but there is no documented creator success cadence, check-in frequency, or retention SOP (§9 🔲 GAP).
